stage.name: Swapping & Bridging (High School)
stage.description: DEX mechanics, AMM pricing, liquidity routing, bridge architecture, wrapped tokens, cross-chain fees, and impermanent loss in swaps.

elements.content:
When you swap on a DEX like Uniswap, you are trading against a liquidity pool — not another person. The pool holds two tokens (e.g. ETH + USDC). The price is set by the ratio: x × y = k. You add one token, the other comes out, and the ratio adjusts.


A DEX aggregator (like 1inch) routes your swap across multiple pools simultaneously to find the best price. Instead of one pool, it might split your trade across five to minimize slippage.

🫙 Liquidity Pool
The reserve you trade against

💰 LP Fee
0.05–1% per swap to LPs

actions.text: ⚙️ How DEX Swaps Actually Work
The mechanics behind every decentralized swap

When you swap on a DEX like Uniswap, you are trading against a liquidity pool — not another person. The pool holds two tokens (e.g. ETH + USDC). The price is set by the ratio: x × y = k . You add one token, the other comes out, and the ratio adjusts. A DEX aggregator (like 1inch) routes your swap across multiple pools simultaneously to find the best price. Instead of one pool, it might split your trade across five to minimize slippage.
🫙 Liquidity Pool. The reserve you trade against
📐 x×y=k. Constant product formula
🔀 Aggregator. Routes across multiple pools
💰 LP Fee. 0.05–1% per swap to LPs

elements.content: Why large swaps get worse prices — and how routing fixes it

Price impact is the effect your trade has on the pool price. A small swap barely moves the ratio. A large swap significantly shifts it — you end up paying more per token as the pool rebalances against you.


Formula: the deeper the pool (more liquidity), the lower your price impact. A $1M pool can absorb a $10k swap with minimal impact. A $50k pool would move dramatically on that same trade.


Pro tip: always check the price impact % before confirming a swap. Above 1% is high. Above 3% is a red flag for smaller tokens.

📉 Price Impact
Your trade moves the pool ratio

actions.text: 🔀 Liquidity Routing & Price Impact
Why large swaps get worse prices — and how routing fixes it

Price impact is the effect your trade has on the pool price. A small swap barely moves the ratio. A large swap significantly shifts it — you end up paying more per token as the pool rebalances against you. Formula: the deeper the pool (more liquidity), the lower your price impact. A $1M pool can absorb a $10k swap with minimal impact. A $50k pool would move dramatically on that same trade. Pro tip: always check the price impact % before confirming a swap. Above 1% is high. Above 3% is a red flag for smaller tokens.
📉 Price Impact. Your trade moves the pool ratio
💧 Deep Pool. Large pool = low impact
🏜️ Shallow Pool. Small pool = high impact
⚠️ 3%+ Impact. Red flag — find more liquidity

elements.content: How bridges lock, mint, and release tokens across chains

There are two main bridge designs:


Lock & Mint — tokens are locked in a smart contract on the source chain. Equivalent wrapped tokens are minted on the destination chain. To return: burn the wrapped tokens, unlock the originals. This is how WBTC (Wrapped Bitcoin) works on Ethereum.


Liquidity Pool Bridge — instead of locking/minting, the bridge uses pre-funded liquidity pools on both chains. You deposit on Chain A, someone withdraws from a pool on Chain B. Faster but requires deep liquidity on both sides.

⛓️ Canonical Bridge
Official chain bridge, slowest but safest

⚡ Third-Party
Faster but adds smart contract risk

actions.text: 🌉 Bridge Architecture
How bridges lock, mint, and release tokens across chains

There are two main bridge designs: Lock & Mint — tokens are locked in a smart contract on the source chain. Equivalent wrapped tokens are minted on the destination chain. To return: burn the wrapped tokens, unlock the originals. This is how WBTC (Wrapped Bitcoin) works on Ethereum. Liquidity Pool Bridge — instead of locking/minting, the bridge uses pre-funded liquidity pools on both chains. You deposit on Chain A, someone withdraws from a pool on Chain B. Faster but requires deep liquidity on both sides.
🔒 Lock & Mint. WBTC model — most common
🌊 LP Bridge. Pre-funded pools, faster
⛓️ Canonical Bridge. Official chain bridge, slowest but safest
⚡ Third-Party. Faster but adds smart contract risk

elements.content: What they are, why they exist, and what the risks are

A wrapped token is a representation of one asset on a different blockchain. WBTC is Bitcoin on Ethereum. WETH is Ethereum on other chains. They maintain a 1:1 peg with the original.


Why this matters: the wrapped token is only as safe as the bridge that issued it. If the bridge is hacked and the locked originals are stolen, the wrapped tokens become worthless instantly. This is what happened in the Ronin bridge hack ($625M stolen) and the Wormhole exploit ($320M).

🎁 Wrapped
Represents original token on new chain

actions.text: 🪙 Wrapped Tokens Explained
What they are, why they exist, and what the risks are

A wrapped token is a representation of one asset on a different blockchain. WBTC is Bitcoin on Ethereum. WETH is Ethereum on other chains. They maintain a 1:1 peg with the original. Why this matters: the wrapped token is only as safe as the bridge that issued it. If the bridge is hacked and the locked originals are stolen, the wrapped tokens become worthless instantly. This is what happened in the Ronin bridge hack ($625M stolen) and the Wormhole exploit ($320M).
🎁 Wrapped. Represents original token on new chain
1:1 Peg. Should equal original in value
🔗 Bridge Risk. Hack = wrapped token worthless
📊 Examples. WBTC, WETH, WXRP

scenes.title: 💸 Cross-Chain Fees & Impermanent Loss in Swaps

elements.content: 💸 Cross-Chain Fees & Impermanent Loss in Swaps
The true cost of bridging and swapping

Every bridge or swap has multiple cost layers that beginners miss:


Source chain gas — fee to initiate the bridge transaction. Bridge fee — the protocol's cut (usually 0.05–0.3%). Destination chain gas — fee to receive on the other side. For Ethereum mainnet bridges, total costs can easily exceed $30–50. For XRPL, total fees are under $0.01.


Impermanent loss also applies if you are providing liquidity to a bridge's LP pool — the same divergence risk as any AMM pool.

actions.text: 💸 Cross-Chain Fees & Impermanent Loss in Swaps
The true cost of bridging and swapping

Every bridge or swap has multiple cost layers that beginners miss: Source chain gas — fee to initiate the bridge transaction. Bridge fee — the protocol's cut (usually 0.05–0.3%). Destination chain gas — fee to receive on the other side. For Ethereum mainnet bridges, total costs can easily exceed $30–50. For XRPL, total fees are under $0.01. Impermanent loss also applies if you are providing liquidity to a bridge's LP pool — the same divergence risk as any AMM pool.
⛽ Source Gas. Fee on originating chain
🌉 Bridge Fee. Protocol cut 0.05–0.3%
⛽ Dest Gas. Fee on receiving chain
💸 XRPL. Total fees under $0.01

questions.question: What formula governs AMM swap pricing?

options.label: p = supply / demand
x × y = k
price = fees / volume
APY = rewards / TVL

questions.analysis: The constant product formula x × y = k ensures that as you buy one token (reducing its reserve), the other token's quantity must increase proportionally to keep the product constant. This automatically adjusts the price — no order book needed.

questions.question: What is a wrapped token?

options.label: A token with extra security features
A representation of one token on a different blockchain
A stablecoin pegged to another crypto
A governance token for bridges

questions.analysis: Wrapped tokens (like WBTC or WETH) represent an asset on a chain where it doesn't natively exist. They maintain a 1:1 peg with the original, backed by locked assets held by the bridge. If the bridge is exploited, wrapped tokens can lose their backing.

questions.question: What made the Ronin bridge hack possible?

options.label: Slippage was set too high
Five of nine validator keys were compromised
The wrapped tokens were minted incorrectly
The DEX aggregator routed poorly

questions.analysis: Ronin used a 5-of-9 multisig where Axie Infinity controlled 4 validators. The attacker compromised all 4 plus one external validator — reaching the 5-of-9 threshold. This centralization made the entire $625M vulnerable to a single coordinated attack.

questions.question: What is price impact in a DEX swap?

options.label: The fee charged by the protocol
How much your trade moves the pool's price
The difference between CEX and DEX prices
Gas cost on the destination chain

questions.analysis: Price impact is the percentage by which your trade shifts the token ratio in the pool. Large trades against small pools have high impact. Always check the price impact before confirming — above 1% is significant, above 3% is a red flag for smaller tokens.

questions.question: What is a DEX aggregator?

options.label: A centralized exchange that lists DEX tokens
A tool that routes your swap across multiple pools for the best price
A bridge that connects two DEXs on different chains
A smart contract that stores liquidity pool rewards

questions.analysis: Aggregators like 1inch split your order across multiple pools simultaneously to minimize slippage and find the best execution price. On large trades, this can save significant amounts compared to trading against a single pool.

questions.question: What is impermanent loss in a swap liquidity pool?

options.label: Gas fees lost on a failed transaction
Loss from providing liquidity when token prices diverge from deposit ratio
The bridge fee on cross-chain transfers
Slippage from a large market order

questions.analysis: When you provide liquidity and token prices move relative to each other, the AMM rebalances your position — leaving you with more of the fallen token and less of the risen one. Compared to just holding, you end up worse off. Stablecoin pairs minimize IL since both prices stay close to $1.

questions.question: Why does bridging on Ethereum mainnet cost more than bridging on XRPL?

options.label: XRPL has faster validators
Ethereum charges per-computation gas fees that can spike with congestion — XRPL charges a flat fee under $0.01
XRPL bridges are subsidized by Ripple
Ethereum bridges require more validator signatures

questions.analysis: Ethereum's gas model charges for every computational operation, and fees spike during congestion — a bridge transaction can cost $30–$100. XRPL charges a fixed 'drops' fee (fractions of XRP) regardless of network load, making it ideal for high-frequency or small-value cross-chain operations.
